metric_value: By Peter Kastner, the Aberdeen Group (InternetWeek); EVP and Chief Research Officer for the Consumer Digital Technology Practice at Aberdeen Group.
Aberdeen/InternetWeek survey completed by over 250 readers managing about 750,000 business PCs; top-10 respondents manage over 550,000 users.
Six deals of 10,000+ PCs totaling 70,000 PCs were done in the last twelve months.
Three companies are now buying 30,000 PCs each and another handful of big orders bring the forward-looking total to 160,000.
Judging by Intel's buoyant Q3 guidance and capacity-driven memory price increases in the past couple of months, it's fair to say the buying drought of the past couple of years is fading away.
notes: Kastner reading of Intel Q3 guidance

metric_value: About 43% of desktops and 54% of laptops are replaced with a philosophy of 'when the need is justified', 'when they break', or 'when a new application requires it'.
The schedule is 36 months (desktops), converging with ~42 months for laptops.
Get rid of those Windows 9x and NT liabilities before the software breaks for good, probably with a virus or worm attack.

Those who replace PCs on a catch-as-catch-can basis also experience higher costs of ownership, according to my research, due to higher support costs. The TCO gap will widen further as the benefits of stable image platforms are not attained by this class.

Industries with the stingiest Yankees are healthcare and retail. Manufacturing is around the average, and financial services and - gasp - governments are least likely to wait for something to go wrong.
Global corporate PC spending recovered strongly 2004-2007; 3-year replacement cycle became the de facto corporate standard through the 2010s.
